threats.title: rentalWallet Excluded from _deriveRentalOrderHash Enables Stop-Rent Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function omits order.rentalWallet from its abi.encode call despite the EIP-712 type string declaring 'address rentalWallet'. This means any caller of stopRent can supply a RentalOrder with a different rentalWallet that produces an identical hash to the stored active order. For ERC1155 tokens rented across multiple protocol-deployed safes, an attacker can redirect reclamation to a different wallet, draining its assets while leaving the original wallet's guard protections permanently stale.
threats.investigation_rationale: The encoding gap between the type string and actual encoding is verifiable by code inspection. The _deriveRentalOrderHash encodes seaportOrderHash, items, hooks, orderType, lender, renter, startTimestamp, endTimestamp but skips rentalWallet. Since stopRent accepts a user-supplied RentalOrder and verifies only the hash against storage, the rentalWallet field is entirely under the caller's control.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode skips from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp, omitting order.rentalWallet despite EIP-712 type string including 'address rentalWallet'

preconditions: An active rental order exists in Storage.orders
For full exploitation: ERC1155 token with same token+id rented to multiple protocol-deployed safes
Rental is expired (BASE) or caller is lender (PAY) to pass _validateRentalCanBeStoped

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Caller provides RentalOrder with altered rentalWallet; hash matches stored hash because rentalWallet is excluded from computation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any caller can invoke stopRent with manipulated order data
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - exists for all orders throughout their rental lifetime and after expiry

operational_questions: Can two different protocol-deployed safes hold the same ERC1155 token+id via separate rentals?
Does removeRentals' rentedAssets decrement succeed when using wrong-wallet rentalIds?
What happens to the original safe's guard protections after the wrong rental is stopped?
validation_requirements: Construct two ERC1155 rentals with same token+id to different safes
Call stopRent for order A with rentalWallet set to safe B
Verify safe B loses assets and safe A's rental becomes permanently unstoppable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create two ERC1155 rentals with overlapping token+id to different safes
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for the target rental to expire
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call stopRent with the target order but substitute rentalWallet to victim safe
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim safe's assets are reclaimed to wrong lender; original safe's assets permanently locked

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Rental tracking permanently corrupted for affected safes
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Assets in victim safe stolen; original safe assets permanently locked behind stale guard
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade to Storage module to fix stale rentedAssets entries
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ERC1155 rental stops, rental asset tracking, guard enforcement

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single transaction, requires knowledge of active rental details (public via events)
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs to construct valid RentalOrder struct with altered rentalWallet
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After rental expiry for BASE orders; anytime for PAY order lenders

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: stopRent calls where rentalWallet differs from original order's rentalWallet
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple ERC1155 rentals created for same token+id across wallets

threats.title: orderType and emittedExtraData Excluded from _deriveOrderMetadataHash Allow Fulfiller Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveOrderMetadataHash function only includes rentDuration and hooks in its encoding, despite the EIP-712 type string declaring 'uint8 orderType, uint256 rentDuration, Hook[] hooks, bytes emittedExtraData'. The fulfiller can change the orderType field (e.g., BASE to PAY) without invalidating the zoneHash check. While current item validation in _convertToItems provides secondary defense against type switching, the missing commitment breaks the security model. The emittedExtraData field can be freely manipulated to inject arbitrary event data.
threats.investigation_rationale: The metadata hash encodes only _ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, and keccak256(hookHashes), omitting metadata.orderType and metadata.emittedExtraData entirely. This is verifiable by code inspection and creates a direct cryptographic bypass of the lender's signed intent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode contains only _ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(hookHashes) — missing metadata.orderType as first field and keccak256(metadata.emittedExtraData) as last field

preconditions: A valid Seaport order exists with Create policy as zone
Fulfiller has a valid protocol signer signature for the rent payload
Fulfiller can modify the OrderMetadata before submitting to Seaport

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fulfiller alters orderType or emittedExtraData in metadata without invalidating zoneHash
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any fulfiller can manipulate uncommitted fields
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every rental creation — the metadata hash never commits orderType

operational_questions: Can changing orderType from BASE to PAY bypass item validation in _convertToItems?
What downstream effects does arbitrary emittedExtraData injection have on off-chain systems?
If new order types are added, would this become directly exploitable?
validation_requirements: Attempt to change orderType in fulfillment and verify zoneHash still matches
Test item validation as secondary defense against each orderType permutation
Verify emittedExtraData can be freely set without hash mismatch

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain valid protocol signer signature for a rental creation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Verify zoneHash check passes with modified orderType
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If item validation doesn't catch the mismatch, order processes with wrong type and payment flow

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Lender's signed order intent can be subverted by fulfiller
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential payment flow reversal if item validation gaps exist; arbitrary event data injection affects indexers
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix hash computation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rental creation flows, off-chain event processing

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - understanding of EIP-712 and Seaport zone mechanics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Orders where emitted orderType differs from lender's intent

threats.title: Hook Status Disable After Rental Creation Permanently Locks Rental Assets
threats.hypothesis_description: When GUARD_ADMIN disables a hook's onStop bit via updateHookStatus after a rental was created with that hook, all stopRent calls for orders referencing that hook will revert at _removeHooks because STORE.hookOnStop(target) returns false. Since the hooks array is embedded in the user-supplied RentalOrder (not modifiable by protocol), there is no mechanism to stop the rental or reclaim the locked NFTs. The lender's assets are permanently trapped in the rental safe.
threats.investigation_rationale: _removeHooks explicitly checks STORE.hookOnStop(target) and reverts with Shared_DisabledHook if the hook's stop bit is disabled. This check is mandatory for every hook in the order. The hooks are part of the order data signed at creation time and cannot be modified post-creation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!STORE.hookOnStop(target)) { revert Errors.Shared_DisabledHook(target); } — mandatory check with no fallback

preconditions: A rental was created with hooks in the order metadata
GUARD_ADMIN subsequently disables the hook's onStop bit (e.g., due to discovered vulnerability in hook)
Any caller attempts to stop the rental

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Administrative hook status change inadvertently makes active rentals unstoppable
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: GUARD_ADMIN performs legitimate operational action (disabling problematic hook)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once hook is disabled — no recovery mechanism exists

operational_questions: Is there any alternative path to stop a rental that bypasses hook processing?
Can the admin re-enable the hook to allow stopping, then disable again?
How many active rentals could be affected by a single hook status change?
validation_requirements: Create a rental with a hook, disable the hook's onStop bit, attempt to stop the rental
Verify that re-enabling the hook allows the rental to be stopped
Test if there's any emergency mechanism to bypass hooks

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify active rentals that reference hooks with onStop enabled
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: GUARD_ADMIN disables hook status (legitimate admin action or social engineering)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All stopRent calls for affected orders revert permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Lender's NFTs locked in rental safe; ERC20 payments locked in escrow
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Hook status change can happen through normal admin operations

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Admin cannot recover from hook disable decision for active rentals
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All rentals using disabled hook are permanently frozen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires protocol upgrade or re-enabling the hook (which may be unsafe if hook is malicious)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rental stop operations for orders referencing the disabled hook

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 0 for attacker; GUARD_ADMIN role needed for the triggering action
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single admin transaction creates the condition
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After any hook status change that disables onStop

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: updateHookStatus calls that clear the onStop bit while active rentals reference the hook
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Active rentals with hooks that were subsequently disabled

threats.title: Guard Permissive Default Allows Non-Standard NFT Functions to Bypass Rental Protections
threats.hypothesis_description: The Guard's _checkTransaction implements a blocklist of known ERC721/ERC1155 transfer and approval selectors. Any function selector not in this explicit list is allowed by default. NFTs implementing non-standard transfer functions (e.g., EIP-4494 permit, custom burn/transfer mechanisms, or game-specific asset management functions) can be called freely from the rental safe, potentially allowing the renter to transfer or approve rented assets through unblocked selectors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _checkTransaction function uses if/else-if chains for specific selectors. The final else block only checks setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom, and setGuard. Any other selector passes through unchecked. This means custom NFT functions like permit(), transferWithAuthorization(), or game-specific move/equip functions that transfer ownership are not blocked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Explicit if/else-if chain for known selectors; unmatched selectors in final else only check 3 additional selectors, then allow transaction to proceed

preconditions: Rented NFT implements a non-standard function that enables transfer or approval
The function's selector is not in the Guard's blocklist
No hook is registered for the token contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Renter calls non-standard function on rented NFT; Guard allows because selector is not blocked
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - depends solely on the rented NFT's interface
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Entire rental duration for any NFT with non-standard transfer/approval mechanisms

operational_questions: Which popular NFT collections implement non-standard transfer or approval functions?
Does EIP-4494 permit() allow bypassing the Guard for ERC721 tokens?
Are there game NFTs with custom transfer mechanisms that would bypass the Guard?
validation_requirements: Deploy an NFT with a custom transfer function (non-standard selector)
Rent it via the protocol, then call the custom function from the rental safe
Verify the Guard allows the transaction and the NFT leaves the safe

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify rented NFT that implements non-standard transfer (e.g., EIP-4494 permit)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: From rental safe, call the non-standard function to approve attacker or transfer asset
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker transfers NFT out of safe via the approval/transfer mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: NFT leaves rental safe; stopRent can still be called but reclaim fails
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - renter acts alone using the rental safe

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lender's rented NFT stolen from rental safe
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: NFT is irrecoverable unless returned voluntarily or via legal action
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rentals of NFTs with non-standard transfer mechanisms

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single transaction from rental safe

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transactions from rental safes calling non-standard selectors on rented tokens
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rented NFTs leaving safes without stopRent being called
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targeted rental of NFTs known to have non-standard functions

threats.title: PAYEE Orders Have No On-Chain Storage Footprint Making Them Untrackable and Unstoppable
threats.hypothesis_description: In _rentFromZone, the PAYEE order type falls through the 'if (isBaseOrder || isPayOrder)' block entirely, meaning no order hash is stored, no rentedAssets are updated, no deposit is increased, and no hooks are processed. PAYEE orders create zero on-chain protocol state. If the corresponding PAY order fails or the PAYEE side's assets need recovery, there is no protocol mechanism to track or stop the PAYEE portion independently.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _rentFromZone function only processes storage updates inside the if block that checks for BASE or PAY orders. PAYEE orders pass through _convertToItems validation but skip the entire storage/escrow/hook processing block. The validateOrder function still returns success to Seaport, meaning assets transfer but the protocol has no record.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()) { /* all storage, escrow, hooks */ } — PAYEE orders skip entirely

preconditions: A PAYEE order is fulfilled via Seaport
The order specifies the Create policy as its zone

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: PAYEE order validated by zone but creates zero on-chain state

operational_questions: Are PAYEE orders always atomically matched with PAY orders in Seaport?
Can a PAYEE order be fulfilled independently without its PAY counterpart?
If the PAY order fails, what happens to assets received via the PAYEE fulfillment?
validation_requirements: Create a PAYEE order and verify no storage entries are created
Attempt to stop a PAYEE-only fulfillment and verify it fails
Test if PAYEE assets can be independently recovered

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Fulfill only the PAYEE portion if Seaport allows independent fulfillment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Assets transfer to PAYEE fulfiller with no protocol tracking
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol has no mechanism to reclaim or track these assets
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Depends on Seaport's order matching semantics

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Protocol loses tracking of PAYEE-side assets
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Assets unrecoverable if PAY side fails
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No protocol mechanism exists; would require direct negotiation or legal action

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: validateOrder calls for PAYEE orders with no corresponding addRentals
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: PAYEE fulfillments without matching PAY order storage entries

threats.title: Guard Blocks All Transactions with Less Than 4 Bytes of Data Trapping ETH in Rental Safes
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkTransaction reverts with GuardPolicy_FunctionSelectorRequired if data.length < 4. This blocks all pure ETH transfers from rental safes (which have empty calldata). Any ETH received by a rental safe (e.g., game rewards, airdrops, accidental sends) becomes permanently trapped because the safe cannot initiate a simple ETH transfer to an EOA.
threats.investigation_rationale: The guard requires data.length >= 4 before any further processing. Pure ETH sends use empty data (length 0). The safe owner cannot construct a valid simple ETH transfer that passes this check. While workarounds exist (calling a helper contract with data), this represents a permanent loss risk for ETH sent to rental safes.
preconditions: A rental safe receives ETH (via game rewards, direct send, etc.)
Safe owner attempts to send ETH out with empty calldata

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Any ETH transfer attempt from rental safe with empty data reverts at guard
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for all rental safes with the guard active

operational_questions: Can the safe owner use a whitelisted contract to forward ETH as a workaround?
Do any game or DeFi integrations send ETH to rental safes?
Is there a protocol mechanism to recover trapped ETH after rental ends?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to a rental safe and attempt to transfer it out with empty calldata
Verify the guard reverts the transaction
Test if any workaround exists via contracts or fallback functions

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Send ETH to a rental safe (or observe game sending ETH rewards)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attempt to send ETH from safe — guard blocks all empty-data transactions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: ETH remains trapped until rental expires and guard is removed (if guard is ever removed)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If rental safe is permanent, ETH is permanently lost

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: ETH locked in rental safes; user funds at risk
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Potentially recoverable after rental end if guard is removed; otherwise permanent
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ETH-related operations from rental safes

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time ETH is sent to a rental safe

threats.title: Active Hook Completely Bypasses Guard's Standard Transaction Checks
threats.hypothesis_description: When Guard.checkTransaction finds an active hook for the target contract, it calls _forwardToHook and skips _checkTransaction entirely. If the hook's onTransaction implementation doesn't replicate all guard protections (blocking transfers, approvals, module changes, guard removal), the renter can execute any prohibited operation through the hooked contract. A single buggy or incomplete hook effectively disables all guard protections for that target.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code structure is: if (hook active) { _forwardToHook } else { _checkTransaction }. These are mutually exclusive paths. The hook has full responsibility for security when active. There is no minimum security requirement enforced on hooks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) { _forwardToHook(...); } else { _checkTransaction(...); } — mutually exclusive, hook path skips all standard checks

preconditions: GUARD_ADMIN has registered an active hook for a target contract
The hook's onTransaction doesn't enforce the same protections as _checkTransaction
Renter initiates a transaction to the hooked target

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Transaction to hooked target bypasses all selector-based restrictions
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: GUARD_ADMIN must have registered the hook (legitimate admin action)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Entire duration while hook is active for the target

operational_questions: Do any deployed hooks replicate the full _checkTransaction protection set?
Can a hook for a staking contract inadvertently allow NFT transfers?
Is there a standard hook template that enforces minimum security guarantees?
validation_requirements: Register a hook that doesn't block transfer selectors
Attempt to transfer rented NFTs through the hooked contract
Verify the Guard does not apply _checkTransaction when hook is active

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target contract with active hook that has incomplete security checks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: From rental safe, call target with transfer/approval selector
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Hook's onTransaction doesn't revert; Guard's _checkTransaction never runs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Rented NFT transferred out of safe

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Rented NFTs stolen from rental safes
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rentals where target contracts have active hooks

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs to identify hooked targets with weak security

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transactions to hooked targets that include transfer selectors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: NFTs leaving rental safes through hooked contract interactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targeted exploitation of specific hooked contracts

threats.title: Whitelisted Delegate Call Target Can Overwrite Guard and Module Storage Slots
threats.hypothesis_description: The Guard allows delegate calls to whitelisted addresses. In delegatecall context, the called contract executes with the Safe's storage. A whitelisted contract (e.g., MultiSend) could directly overwrite the Safe's guard storage slot (keccak256('guard_manager.guard.address')) or module linked list, removing all rental protections. The empty checkAfterExecution provides no post-execution verification that guard and module state were preserved.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guard.checkTransaction allows delegatecall if STORE.whitelistedDelegates(to) is true. In delegatecall, the target contract has full SSTORE access to the Safe's storage. A MultiSend-like contract or any contract with arbitrary call capabilities could batch operations that overwrite critical safe configuration slots. checkAfterExecution is empty, providing no post-execution integrity check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (operation == Enum.Operation.DelegateCall && !STORE.whitelistedDelegates(to)) { revert; } — allows delegatecall to whitelisted targets with no post-execution verification

preconditions: ADMIN_ADMIN has whitelisted a delegate call target (e.g., Gnosis MultiSend)
The whitelisted target allows arbitrary subcalls or SSTORE operations
Rental safe has active rentals

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Delegatecall to whitelisted contract modifies Safe internal storage slots
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Delegate must be whitelisted by ADMIN_ADMIN
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Entire duration while delegate is whitelisted

operational_questions: Is Gnosis MultiSend whitelisted as a delegate call target?
Can MultiSend batch calls include SSTORE to the guard storage slot?
Does the guard slot layout match well-known Gnosis Safe storage positions?
validation_requirements: Whitelist MultiSend, delegatecall from safe with payload overwriting guard slot
Verify guard is removed after delegatecall
Attempt to transfer rented NFTs from now-unguarded safe

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify whitelisted delegate call targets that allow arbitrary calls
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Construct delegatecall payload that overwrites guard storage slot to address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute from rental safe — guard allows delegatecall to whitelisted target
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Guard is removed; freely transfer all rented NFTs out of safe

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All rented NFTs in the safe can be stolen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: NFTs irrecoverable; guard must be reinstalled via separate mechanism
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rental protections for affected safes

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: High - needs knowledge of Safe storage layout

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Delegatecall transactions that modify Safe guard or module storage
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Guard address changing to zero on rental safes
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Delegatecall followed by NFT transfers from safe

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Inflate Escrow Tracked Balance Causing Settlement Failures
threats.hypothesis_description: PaymentEscrow.increaseDeposit records the nominal amount from the order, but fee-on-transfer ERC20 tokens deliver less than the specified amount. This inflates balanceOf[token] relative to the actual token balance. When settlePayment attempts to transfer the full pre-fee amount, it may fail due to insufficient actual balance, permanently locking lender's NFTs because stopRent cannot complete.
threats.investigation_rationale: increaseDeposit adds the full item.amount to balanceOf[token]. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the actual received amount is less. After multiple orders, the tracked balance exceeds the real balance. During settlement, _safeTransfer attempts to transfer amounts that don't exist, reverting the entire stopRent transaction.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol signer does not filter fee-on-transfer tokens when signing rental payloads
threats.config_evidence: Documentation states FOT tokens are not supported but no on-chain enforcement exists in Create.sol or PaymentEscrow.sol

proof_of_signal.pattern: _increaseDeposit(token, amount) records nominal amount; no check that actual received matches

preconditions: Protocol signer signs a rental payload using a fee-on-transfer ERC20
The token charges a transfer fee reducing actual amount received by escrow
A subsequent rental stop triggers settlement

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Escrow tracked balance diverges from actual balance after FOT token deposits
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Protocol signer must authorize FOT token rental (off-chain)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After any FOT token rental creation

operational_questions: Does the protocol signer have a token allowlist that excludes FOT tokens?
Can skim() recover from negative delta (trueBalance < syncedBalance)?
Would the overflow in skim() permanently DoS all operations for that token?
validation_requirements: Create rental with FOT token, verify balanceOf[token] > actual balance
Attempt to stop the rental and observe settlement revert
Test skim() behavior when trueBalance < syncedBalance (expected underflow revert)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Request protocol signer to authorize a rental with a fee-on-transfer ERC20 payment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Fulfill the order — escrow records full amount but receives less
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When rental expires, stopRent attempts settlement but _safeTransfer fails
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Lender's NFTs permanently locked in rental safe; escrow balance corrupted
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires protocol signer to not filter the token

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Affected rentals permanently unstoppable; lender NFTs locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires manual intervention or contract upgrade to fix accounting
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rentals and settlements involving the FOT token

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Low - standard rental creation with FOT token

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rental creation with known fee-on-transfer tokens

threats.title: CEI Violation in stopRent Enables Cross-Order Reentrancy via NFT Callbacks
threats.hypothesis_description: In stopRent, external calls (_removeHooks, _reclaimRentedItems with NFT safeTransferFrom callbacks, ESCRW.settlePayment with ERC20 transfers) execute before STORE.removeRentals. During NFT reclamation, the lender receives an onERC721Received or onERC1155Received callback while the order is still marked active in Storage. A malicious lender contract could re-enter stopRent or stopRentBatch for other orders, potentially causing double-settlement of shared ERC20 tokens or corrupting rental state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The execution order in stopRent is: validate → accumulate → hooks → reclaim (external callbacks) → settle (ERC20 transfers) → remove storage. During the reclaim phase, the lender gets a callback with the order still in Storage.orders. If the lender re-enters with a different order that shares tokens in the escrow, payment settlement could occur twice for the same escrow funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _reclaimRentedItems(order) → ESCRW.settlePayment(order) → STORE.removeRentals(...); external callbacks in reclaim occur before storage update

preconditions: Lender is a smart contract implementing onERC721Received or onERC1155Received
Multiple active rental orders share the same ERC20 payment token in escrow
All relevant orders are expired or caller is lender (PAY orders)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: NFT callback during reclaim triggers re-entrant stopRent for different order
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - lender controls the callback contract
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any stopRent execution where lender is a contract

operational_questions: Can a re-entrant stopRent call for a different order succeed during the callback?
Would the escrow's balanceOf underflow if two settlements occur for the same token?
Does the Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic prevent double-drain or cause the outer tx to revert?
validation_requirements: Deploy lender contract that re-enters stopRent in onERC721Received callback
Create two orders with shared ERC20 token
Attempt re-entrant stop and observe escrow balance effects

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create multiple rental orders as lender using a malicious contract address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call stopRent for order A; during NFT callback, re-enter stopRent for order B
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Both settlements execute against the same escrow balance; potential double-drain if balance allows
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Lender must be a custom contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Escrow balance corruption; other users' settlements may fail
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rental stops sharing the same ERC20 token in escrow

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Nested stopRent calls in the same transaction
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Escrow balance underflows or unexpected settlement patterns
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Lender contracts with complex callback logic

threats.title: Pro-Rata Calculation Overflow Blocks Early Settlement of PAY Orders
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calculatePaymentProRata, the numerator is computed as (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000. For large ERC20 amounts and long elapsed times, this multiplication can overflow uint256 even with Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic, causing revert. This prevents the lender from stopping a PAY order early, forcing them to wait until expiry for full settlement (which bypasses pro-rata calculation).
threats.investigation_rationale: uint256 max is ~1.15e77. With amount = 1e30 (large token supply), elapsedTime = 1e8 (3+ years in seconds), (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000 = 1e41 which is safe. However, with extremely high decimals tokens or synthetic amounts approaching 1e60+, overflow occurs. More realistically, amount = 1e38 (10^20 tokens with 18 decimals) and elapsedTime = 1e7 gives 1e48, still safe. Practical overflow requires very large amounts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 numerator = (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000; — triple multiplication with no overflow guard

preconditions: A PAY order exists with extremely large ERC20 payment amount
The lender attempts to stop the order before expiry (triggering pro-rata calculation)
(amount * elapsedTime * 1000) exceeds uint256 max

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Settlement reverts with overflow for large-amount PAY orders stopped early
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During early stop of high-value PAY orders

operational_questions: What is the maximum practical amount * elapsedTime product before overflow?
Are there ERC20 tokens with enough supply to trigger this?
Does the overflow only affect pro-rata (early stop) or also full settlement?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact overflow boundary for the multiplication
Create PAY order with maximum feasible amount and attempt early stop
Verify that full settlement (after expiry) bypasses the vulnerable code path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create PAY order with extremely large ERC20 amount (use high-supply low-value token)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Rent NFTs to victim via PAY order
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When lender tries to stop early, pro-rata calculation overflows and reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Lender must wait for full rental duration, renter benefits from extended use
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Attacker creates the order as lender or renter

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lender cannot exercise early stop right for affected PAY orders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Self-resolving at expiry (full settlement bypasses pro-rata)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Early stop of high-value PAY orders

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted stopRent calls with arithmetic overflow for PAY orders
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large-amount PAY orders that cannot be stopped early
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Orders created with unusually large ERC20 amounts

threats.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Forks
threats.hypothesis_description: The Signer contract computes _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR once at deployment using block.chainid and stores it as immutable. _CHAIN_ID is stored but never checked at runtime in _recoverSignerFromPayload. After a chain fork where block.chainid changes on one fork, valid rental creation signatures become replayable on both chains because the same domain separator is used, despite the chain IDs diverging.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard EIP-712 implementations (e.g., OpenZeppelin) recompute the domain separator if block.chainid differs from the stored chain ID. The Signer contract stores _CHAIN_ID but never compares it to block.chainid before using _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR. After a fork, signatures intended for one chain work on both.
preconditions: A chain fork occurs where block.chainid changes on the forked chain
Valid protocol signer signatures exist from before the fork
Both chains have the same protocol deployment

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Same signature validates on both chains after fork
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Post-fork, for all existing valid signatures

operational_questions: Is a chain fork realistically expected for the target chains?
Could signature expiration mitigate replay (how long are signatures valid)?
Would replayed rentals on the forked chain cause actual harm?
validation_requirements: Verify _CHAIN_ID is never compared to block.chainid in any code path
Test signature validity after simulated chain fork
Assess if existing signature expiration limits the replay window

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Collect valid rental creation signatures from the original chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or trigger a chain fork
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Replay collected signatures on the forked chain
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Unauthorized rentals created on the forked chain using replayed signatures
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires a chain fork event (rare, out of attacker's control)

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Protocol signer can be rotated; requires redeployment on forked chain
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All rental creation on the forked chain

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Duplicate rental creation signatures on different chain IDs
